profile.summary: A Lead UX Designer sets experience direction for a product area, turns user and business needs into clear interaction patterns, and raises design quality across the team. They usually balance hands-on design with coaching, critique, and cross-functional alignment with product, engineering, research, content, and stakeholders.

known_challenges.text: Balancing hands-on design work with coaching, reviews, and stakeholder alignment.
Protecting user experience quality when delivery pressure pushes for quick fixes.
Creating consistency across teams without becoming a bottleneck for decisions.
Handling the emotional load of constant feedback, critique, and visible decisions.
Influencing product and engineering peers when design authority is informal rather than explicit.

recommended_question_themes.why: Lead UX Designers often carry invisible work in critiques, alignment, and mentoring that can crowd out deep design time.
This role succeeds through influence with product and engineering, so friction here quickly affects delivery and quality.
They are often responsible for patterns, standards, and experience coherence across a wider surface area.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Team growth through feedback and critique
recommended_question_themes.why: A lead usually shapes how other designers improve, not just the work they produce personally.
They need enough research and evidence to steer priorities, not just polish screens after decisions are made.

terminology.meaning: The path a user takes through a task.
A low-detail layout used to explore structure.
How screens, states, and behaviors work together.
Designing so more people can use the product.
A structured review of work and reasoning.
Shared components, patterns, and rules for consistency.
Getting key partners to support a direction.
Choosing what design work matters most now.
Early work to understand problems and opportunities.
Watching users try tasks to find friction.
Ongoing learning with users during product development.
Passing design intent and details to engineering.
Experience issues left unresolved due to constraints.

listen_for: Whether they talk about outcomes and user problems, not just screens and deliverables.
How they describe tension with product or engineering around trade-offs.
Whether they mention coaching other designers or only their own workload.
Shifts from confident design language to fatigue around reviews, meetings, or rework.
Whether user research is something they can influence or something they are blocked from using.
avoid: Treating them like a pure people manager if they are still expected to lead design hands-on.
Reducing design to visual polish instead of product thinking and interaction quality.
Assuming consistency means enforcing one solution everywhere without context.
Pushing them to move faster without acknowledging review load and cross-team influence work.
